title: Mind won't switch off at night
hindi_title: रात में दिमाग शांत न होना
excerpt: When racing thoughts keep you awake every night, Ayurveda points to an excess of vata, the air-and-space energy that governs the mind. Here is what the classical texts say, and what you can do about it.
at_a_glance: Racing bedtime thoughts are a sign of aggravated vata, the air-and-space dosha that governs mental movement.
Late screens, irregular meals and suppressed sleep urges are classic vata triggers named in the classical texts.
A short wind-down routine with warm milk, foot massage and alternate nostril breathing can calm the mind within weeks.

body.heading: why your mind won't settle at night
paragraphs: You lie down, the room is quiet, and then the thoughts begin: tomorrow's meeting, a conversation that went wrong, something you forgot to do. For many people this is simply Tuesday night. You are not anxious in any dramatic sense, your body is tired, but the mind refuses to cooperate. Ayurveda has a name for this state: chittodvega, a restless agitation of the mind, driven by an imbalance of vata dosha.

Vata is the dosha made up of air and space. It governs all movement in the body, including the movement of thoughts. When vata is balanced, the mind is creative and clear. When it is aggravated, thoughts move too fast, skip between subjects and loop back without resolution. The Caraka Samhita describes vata as the force that initiates all mental and physical activity, and also the first cause of disease when it falls out of balance (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Nighttime is a naturally vata-dominant period: the world is still, the nervous system is meant to wind down, but an overloaded vata keeps the mental fan spinning.

body.heading: what aggravates vata in modern life
paragraphs: The classical texts listed the main vata-provoking factors centuries ago, and they map remarkably well onto contemporary habits. Irregular meals, eating cold or dry food, excessive travel, loud stimulating environments and, most relevant today, staying awake past the body's natural sleep window. Caraka notes that suppressing the natural urge to sleep is one of the causes of vata vitiation (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, chapter on natural urges). Scrolling on a bright screen late at night qualifies on several fronts at once: it is stimulating, it delays sleep and the blue light disrupts melatonin production.

Yoga Ratnakara, a later comprehensive text on Ayurvedic therapeutics, reiterates that the mind and the breath are intimately connected, and that whatever disturbs the breath will disturb the mind. Rushed eating, skipped meals, anxiety and irregular routines all create vata imbalance that surfaces most visibly at the time the body most needs to be still. By recognising these triggers, you already have a map for change.

body.heading: a gentle wind-down ritual you can start tonight
paragraphs: Ayurveda recommends a concept called ratricharya, a simple evening routine designed to pacify vata before sleep. The principles are warmth, slowness, regularity and sensory quietness. You do not need to overhaul your life. A handful of consistent habits, practised for two or three weeks, make a measurable difference.

Warm milk prepared with a pinch of nutmeg (jaiphal) has been recommended in Ayurvedic domestic practice for centuries as a mild, calming nervine. Nutmeg in small amounts is considered mildly sedative and vata-pacifying. Drink it about thirty minutes before you intend to sleep. Gentle self-massage of the feet and scalp with warm sesame oil before bathing is another traditional method: sesame is considered the premier oil for vata pacification in the Ashtanga Hridaya (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Even five minutes of this, focussing on the soles of the feet, calms the nervous system noticeably.

Pranayama, specifically nadi shodhana (alternate nostril breathing), is recommended in both Ayurvedic and Yogic texts as a direct tool for balancing vata. Yoga Ratnakara places great emphasis on the regulation of prana as the route to mental stillness. Sit comfortably in bed or on the floor, close the right nostril with the thumb, inhale slowly through the left, then switch and exhale through the right. Repeat for five to ten cycles. It sounds simple because it is, and it works by directly slowing the breath and, with it, the mental pace.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: Occasional sleeplessness driven by overthinking is common and often responds well to lifestyle adjustments. However, if you regularly cannot sleep for more than a few hours despite good habits, if the sleeplessness is accompanied by persistent sadness, anxiety, heart palpitations, breathlessness or chest pain, or if it is significantly affecting your ability to function during the day, please see a qualified doctor or psychiatrist. Chronic insomnia can have medical causes including thyroid disorders, sleep apnea and depression, all of which need proper evaluation. A qualified Ayurvedic vaidya can complement medical care but is not a replacement for it. The information here is general guidance, not a diagnosis or a prescription.

try_this_today.title: Warm nutmeg milk
try_this_today.description: Heat a cup of whole milk until just steaming. Stir in a small pinch of freshly grated nutmeg (no more than one-eighth of a teaspoon). Drink slowly about thirty minutes before sleep.

try_this_today.title: Sesame foot massage
try_this_today.description: Warm a tablespoon of sesame oil between your palms and massage the soles of your feet for five minutes before your evening bath or shower. Focus on the arches and the heels.

try_this_today.title: Nadi shodhana before sleep
try_this_today.description: Sit comfortably, close your right nostril with your thumb and inhale slowly through the left for a count of four. Switch: close the left nostril with your ring finger and exhale through the right for a count of four. Inhale right, exhale left. That is one cycle. Do ten cycles.

try_this_today.title: Fixed sleep window
try_this_today.description: Choose a sleep time and a wake time and keep them the same every day, including weekends. Ayurveda treats regularity as medicine for vata. Even one week of a consistent schedule reduces evening restlessness.

try_this_today.title: Screen curfew
try_this_today.description: Put your phone in another room at least forty-five minutes before sleep. Replace the scroll habit with something tactile and slow: a warm drink, light reading on paper, or the breathing exercise above.

words_to_know.definition: One of the three doshas, made of air and space; governs all movement, including thought.
Restless agitation of the mind; a term used in Ayurvedic texts for mental unrest.
The Ayurvedic evening routine; a set of simple habits to prepare body and mind for sleep.
Alternate nostril breathing; a pranayama practice that balances the nervous system.
Vital life force carried by the breath; closely linked to the movement of vata in the body.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana (on vata, natural urges, and causes of disease) · Yoga Ratnakara, General Ayurvedic therapeutics (prana regulation and mental equilibrium) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (on properties and uses of sesame oil)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
